:::: {.panel-tabset .nav-pills} ## Prison Population
::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

New Court Commits vs. Parole Revocations

While the public often envisions a prison population primarily made up of individuals serving long sentences for serious crimes, the reality is far more nuanced. In particular, the distinction between new court commits—those incarcerated for a new crime—and parole revocations—those returned to prison for violating the conditions of their parole—is a critical one. Understanding the proportion of these two groups within the prison population can provide valuable insights into the effectiveness of the state's criminal justice policies, including sentencing and parole practices.


``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

## Parole Eligibility
::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

How is Parole Eligibility Determined?

An inmate with an in- determinate sentence may be paroled at “any time” after the expiration of their minimum term of incarceration. The minimum term of incarceration can be reduced through time credits. Determinate sentences involve a fixed length of prison time with no eligibility for parole release; however time credits can be applied to the sentence and result in early release when 6/7 of the full sentence is complete. This type of early release is called “conditional release,” and is supervised in the same manner as parole. Inmates may be conditionally released by DOCCS, not the Board of Parole. Determinate sentences also normally require a period of “post-release supervision” which is an additional part of the sentence. Conditional release is also available to those serving indeterminate sentences after they have completed 2/3 of their minimum sentence. Presumptive release exists for certain non-violent offenders who have received a certificate of earned eligibility from the State Commissioner of Corrections and Community Supervision. This allows inmates to return to the community under supervision after 5/6 of their prison term is finished, at the discretion of the Board.


``` ::: ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Parole-Eligible Prison Population

In 2020, there were 1,454 people who were eligible for parole but remained incarcerated for a new crime. This group made up 4% of the prison population.


``` ::: ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

People who are Eligible for Parole

``` ::: ::: ::: {.panel-tabset} ### Race and Ethnicity ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

In 2020, Black, non-Hispanic people made up the largest proportion of those eligible for parole yet still incarcerated for a new criminal offense, comprising 43% of the parole-eligible population serving time for new crimes.

``` ::: ::: ### Gender ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

In 2020, male people made up the largest proportion of those eligible for parole yet still incarcerated for a new criminal offense, comprising 97% of the parole-eligible population serving time for new crimes.

``` ::: ::: ### Age ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

In 2020, people who were between the ages of 25-34 years old made up the largest proportion of those eligible for parole yet still incarcerated for a new criminal offense, comprising 29% of the parole-eligible population serving time for new crimes.

``` ::: ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Predicted Probabilities

Predicted probabilities: Controlling for offense and sentence length, predicted probabilities of release before/at eligibility date. Something like “After controlling for differences in ages, offenses, and sentence length, 66% of White people, 40% of Black people, and 55% of Hispanic people are released before/on parole eligibility date. Similarly, 80% of women and 74% of men were released before/on parole eligibility date
